1:
"And when Rachel saw that she bare Jacob no children, Rachel envied her sister; and said unto Jacob, Give me children, or else I die." The witnesses note Rachel's envy and impatient cry — the crux of her discontent. On "Rachel saw that she bare Jacob no children, Rachel envied her sister," the grammatical voices note the envy. Ellicott and Gill note that "Rachel saw that she bare Jacob no children, Rachel envied her sister" — Rachel, barren while Leah bore four sons (29:31-35), envied her sister; the sin of envy and discontent; the beloved wife (29:30) grieved at her barrenness; the rivalry of the sisters breaking out.

On "Give me children, or else I die," interpreters expound the cry. The puritan Henry and the dominant reading note that Rachel said to Jacob, "Give me children, or else I die" — an impatient, fretful, immoderate demand: her heart set inordinately on children (one comfort lacking embittered all the rest); the discontent that could not be satisfied; the impatience that, as it were, would die without children; Henry notes, "we are very apt to be discontented with what God has appointed us"; the fretting of the envious heart, demanding of Jacob what only God could give (v.2); the sinful, immoderate craving (contrast Hannah's prayer, 1 Samuel 1:10-11, who took her grief to God).

On the demand, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the misdirection: Rachel demanded children of Jacob ("Give me children"), as if her husband could give what God withholds; the misdirected craving (Jacob rightly answers, Am I in God's stead? v.2); the discontent that looked to man, not God; the lesson that children are the gift of God (Psalm 127:3), to be sought of him. The verse displays Rachel's envy and impatient cry.

The witnesses agree the substance: Rachel, envying Leah, cried to Jacob, Give me children, or else I die (Ellicott, Henry, Calvin, Gill). The cry bears its weight as the crux of her discontent: the envy of her sister and the impatient, immoderate demand — her heart set inordinately on children, fretting, as if she would die without them; the discontent that demanded of Jacob what only God could give (v.2; Psalm 127:3); the misdirected craving (contrast Hannah, 1 Samuel 1). The verse displays the discontent crux. The verse marks the envy: of her sister. The verse displays the cry: give me children or I die. The verse displays the impatience: immoderate. The verse displays the misdirection: to Jacob, not God. The cry displays Rachel's envious, fretful heart. The verse displays the Rachel: envying. The verse displays the demand: impatient. The verse displays the children: God's to give. Rachel's envy and impatient cry display a crux: Rachel envied her sister and cried, Give me children, or else I die — the immoderate, fretful demand of the discontented heart, set inordinately on children, demanding of Jacob what only God could give (v.2; Psalm 127:3). The verse displays the envious Rachel, rebuked by Jacob (v.2).
2:
"And Jacob's anger was kindled against Rachel: and he said, Am I in God's stead, who hath withheld from thee the fruit of the womb?" The witnesses note Jacob's reply that children are the gift of God. On "Jacob's anger was kindled against Rachel," the grammatical voices note the displeasure. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Jacob's anger was kindled against Rachel" — Jacob was displeased at Rachel's fretful, misdirected demand (v.1); the just (if not wholly patient) displeasure at her impatience; the husband grieved that she demanded of him what was God's to give; the rebuke of her discontent.

On "Am I in God's stead, who hath withheld from thee the fruit of the womb?" interpreters expound the reply. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note Jacob's answer — "Am I in God's stead, who hath withheld from thee the fruit of the womb?": Jacob disclaimed the power to give children (he is not God); it is God, not man, who opens and shuts the womb (29:31; 1 Samuel 1:5); God had withheld children from Rachel; the children are God's gift (Psalm 127:3), to be sought of him, not demanded of the husband; Jacob's reply, though sharp, rightly directed Rachel to God; the acknowledgment that fruitfulness is in God's hand alone.

On "who hath withheld from thee the fruit of the womb," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the providence: God had withheld the fruit of the womb from Rachel; the sovereign hand of God in giving and withholding children; the lesson for Rachel (and all) to seek of God, in prayer (as she at last did, v.22, God remembered Rachel), what God alone can give; the gift of children in God's hand. The verse displays Jacob's reply that children are the gift of God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the displeasure — "Jacob's anger was kindled against Rachel," the rebuke of her impatience (v.1, Keil, Gill); "Am I in God's stead?" the disclaimer, children God's to give (29:31; Psalm 127:3, Calvin, Gill); "who hath withheld from thee the fruit of the womb," God's sovereign hand (v.22, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the answering Jacob. The verse marks the anger: kindled. The verse displays the reply: am I in God's stead. The verse displays the womb: God withholds. The verse displays the gift: of God. The reply displays Jacob directing Rachel to God. The verse displays the Jacob: answering. The verse displays the God: who gives. The verse displays the children: his gift. Jacob's reply that children are the gift of God displays the truth: am I in God's stead, who hath withheld the fruit of the womb? — Jacob disclaiming the power to give children (God alone opens the womb, 29:31; Psalm 127:3), directing Rachel to seek of God what he withholds. The verse displays the answering Jacob, given Bilhah by Rachel (v.3).
3:
"And she said, Behold my maid Bilhah, go in unto her; and she shall bear upon my knees, that I may also have children by her." The witnesses note Rachel giving Bilhah to bear children for her. On "Behold my maid Bilhah, go in unto her," the grammatical voices note the expedient. Ellicott and Gill note that "Behold my maid Bilhah, go in unto her" — Rachel, still barren, gave her handmaid Bilhah to Jacob to bear children for her (as Sarah gave Hagar, 16:2-3); the expedient of the time (the children of the handmaid reckoned to the mistress); the impatient resort to human means (rather than waiting on God, who would remember her, v.22); the custom followed.

On "she shall bear upon my knees," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the adoption. Clarke and Ellicott note "she shall bear upon my knees" — a token of adoption (the child born on the knees of the mistress, reckoned as hers); Rachel claiming the handmaid's children as her own; the legal fiction of the time; the children counted to Rachel.

On "that I may also have children by her," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the rivalry: "that I may also have children by her" — Rachel sought children (even by the handmaid) to match Leah; the rivalry of the sisters; the impatient expedient (as Sarah's, 16:2, which the witnesses note was not without fault, yet overruled by God); the children of Bilhah (Dan, Naphtali, vv.5-8) reckoned to Rachel. The verse displays Rachel giving Bilhah to bear children for her.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the expedient — "Behold my maid Bilhah, go in unto her," Rachel giving her handmaid (16:2-3, Ellicott, Gill); "she shall bear upon my knees," the adoption (Clarke, Ellicott); "that I may also have children by her," the rivalry and the impatient resort (16:2, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the expedient Rachel. The verse marks the maid: Bilhah. The verse displays the bearing: on her knees. The verse displays the children: reckoned to Rachel. The verse displays the rivalry: with Leah. The expedient displays Rachel seeking children by her handmaid. The verse displays the Rachel: giving Bilhah. The verse displays the children: by the maid. The verse displays the rivalry: continued. Rachel giving Bilhah to bear children for her displays the impatient expedient: go in unto Bilhah, that I may have children by her (16:2-3) — the resort to the handmaid (as Sarah), the children reckoned to Rachel, the rivalry with Leah. The verse displays the expedient Rachel, with Bilhah's son Dan (vv.4-6).
4:
"And she gave him Bilhah her handmaid to wife: and Jacob went in unto her." The very brief verse notes Rachel giving Bilhah to Jacob. The grammatical voices note the act. Gill notes that "she gave him Bilhah her handmaid to wife: and Jacob went in unto her" — Rachel gave Bilhah to Jacob as a (secondary) wife/concubine, and Jacob took her (as Abraham took Hagar, 16:3-4); the carrying out of Rachel's expedient (v.3); the handmaid given to bear children for the mistress; the act done.

On "Bilhah her handmaid to wife," the witnesses note the secondary wife. Gill notes that Bilhah was given "to wife" (a secondary wife/concubine, as Hagar, 16:3); the children to be reckoned to Rachel (v.3); the customary arrangement; the handmaid become a mother of the tribes (Dan, Naphtali, vv.6-8).

On "Jacob went in unto her," the grammatical-historical note the consequence: "Jacob went in unto her" — Jacob took Bilhah; the conception to follow (v.5); the providence (and the human expedient) building the house of Israel; the handmaid's children among the twelve. The verse displays Rachel giving Bilhah to Jacob.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the act — "she gave him Bilhah her handmaid to wife: and Jacob went in unto her," Rachel's expedient carried out (16:3, Gill); "Bilhah her handmaid to wife," the secondary wife (Gill); "Jacob went in unto her," the consequence (v.5, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the giving Rachel. The verse marks the maid: Bilhah. The verse displays the wife: secondary. The verse displays the Jacob: took her. The verse displays the children: to come. The act displays Rachel's expedient carried out. The verse displays the Rachel: giving. The verse displays the Bilhah: to Jacob. The verse displays the providence: building. Rachel giving Bilhah to Jacob displays the expedient carried out: Rachel gave Bilhah to wife, and Jacob went in unto her (16:3) — the handmaid given as a secondary wife, to bear children for Rachel (vv.6-8). The verse displays the conceiving Bilhah (v.5).
5:
"And Bilhah conceived, and bare Jacob a son." The very brief verse notes Bilhah bearing Jacob a son. The grammatical voices note the conception. Gill and Calvin note that "Bilhah conceived, and bare Jacob a son" — Bilhah (Rachel's handmaid) conceived and bore Jacob a son (Dan, v.6); the first child of the handmaid; the children reckoned to Rachel (v.3); the building of Jacob's house by the handmaid; the providence (through the expedient) multiplying the seed.

On "Bilhah conceived," the witnesses note the fruitfulness. Gill notes that Bilhah conceived at once — the handmaid fruitful (while Rachel was barren); the child to be Rachel's (by adoption, v.3); the conception that began the second round of births.

On "bare Jacob a son," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the son: "bare Jacob a son" — the first of Bilhah's two sons (Dan, Naphtali, vv.6-8); the son reckoned to Rachel; the handmaid a mother of the tribes; the house of Israel growing. The verse displays Bilhah bearing Jacob a son.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the conception — "Bilhah conceived, and bare Jacob a son," the handmaid's first son (v.6, Gill, Calvin); "Bilhah conceived," the fruitfulness (Gill); "bare Jacob a son," Dan, reckoned to Rachel (v.3, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the bearing Bilhah. The verse marks the conception: Bilhah's. The verse displays the son: born. The verse displays the Rachel: his mother by adoption. The verse displays the tribes: growing. The conception displays the handmaid bearing for Rachel. The verse displays the Bilhah: bearing. The verse displays the son: Jacob's. The verse displays the house: building. Bilhah bearing Jacob a son displays the handmaid's fruitfulness for Rachel: Bilhah conceived and bare Jacob a son (Dan, v.6) — the child reckoned to Rachel (v.3), the house of Israel growing through the handmaid. The verse displays the bearing Bilhah, whose son Rachel names Dan (v.6).
6:
"And Rachel said, God hath judged me, and hath also heard my voice, and hath given me a son: therefore called she his name Dan." The witnesses note the naming of Dan, God having judged Rachel. On "God hath judged me, and hath also heard my voice," the grammatical voices note the name. Ellicott and Gill note that "Rachel said, God hath judged me, and hath also heard my voice, and hath given me a son: therefore called she his name Dan" — Rachel named the son Dan ("judging," or "he hath judged"), because God had judged her cause (vindicated her, given sentence in her favor) and heard her prayer; the acknowledgment that God had regarded her; the name commemorating God's judging/hearing.

On "God hath judged me," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the vindication. Calvin and Gill note that "God hath judged me" — God had given sentence in Rachel's favor (judged her cause against her barrenness, or against Leah's reproaching, freeing her from the reproach); the divine vindication; Rachel owning God's hand (yet the child by the handmaid); the judging God.

On "hath also heard my voice," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the prayer: "hath also heard my voice" — God had heard Rachel's prayer (so she had prayed, amid her impatience); the answered cry; the name Dan ("judge") commemorating God's judging and hearing; the son who would head the tribe of Dan. The verse displays the naming of Dan.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the name — "God hath judged me... and hath given me a son: therefore called she his name Dan," God judging and hearing (Ellicott, Gill); "God hath judged me," the vindication (Calvin, Gill); "hath also heard my voice," the answered prayer (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the naming Rachel. The verse marks the name: Dan. The verse displays the judging: of God. The verse displays the voice: heard. The verse displays the son: given. The naming displays Rachel owning God's judging. The verse displays the Rachel: naming. The verse displays the Dan: judge. The verse displays the God: who heard. The naming of Dan displays God's judging and hearing: God hath judged me and heard my voice; she called him Dan (judge) — Rachel owning God's vindication and answered prayer, the son who would head the tribe of Dan. The verse displays the naming Rachel, with Bilhah's second son (vv.7-8).
7:
"And Bilhah Rachel's maid conceived again, and bare Jacob a second son." The very brief verse notes Bilhah bearing a second son. The grammatical voices note the conception. Gill notes that "Bilhah Rachel's maid conceived again, and bare Jacob a second son" — Bilhah conceived again and bore Jacob a second son (Naphtali, v.8); the second child of the handmaid, reckoned to Rachel (v.3); the house of Israel growing; the providence multiplying the seed through the handmaid.

On "conceived again," the witnesses note the fruitfulness. Gill notes that Bilhah conceived again — the handmaid fruitful a second time; the child reckoned to Rachel; the second of Bilhah's two sons.

On "bare Jacob a second son," the grammatical-historical note the son: "bare Jacob a second son" — Naphtali (v.8); the second son of the handmaid; the tribe of Naphtali; the gathering of the twelve. The verse displays Bilhah bearing a second son.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the conception — "Bilhah... conceived again, and bare Jacob a second son," Bilhah's second son (v.8, Gill); "conceived again," the fruitfulness (Gill); "bare Jacob a second son," Naphtali (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the bearing Bilhah. The verse marks the conception: again. The verse displays the son: second. The verse displays the Naphtali: to come. The verse displays the tribes: growing. The conception displays the handmaid bearing again for Rachel. The verse displays the Bilhah: bearing. The verse displays the son: second. The verse displays the house: building. Bilhah bearing a second son displays the handmaid's continued fruitfulness: Bilhah conceived again and bare a second son (Naphtali, v.8) — the second child reckoned to Rachel, the house of Israel growing. The verse displays the bearing Bilhah, whose son Rachel names Naphtali (v.8).
8:
"And Rachel said, With great wrestlings have I wrestled with my sister, and I have prevailed: and she called his name Naphtali." The witnesses note the naming of Naphtali, Rachel's wrestlings with her sister. On "With great wrestlings have I wrestled with my sister, and I have prevailed," the grammatical voices note the rivalry. Ellicott and Gill note that "With great wrestlings have I wrestled with my sister, and I have prevailed: and she called his name Naphtali" — Rachel named the son Naphtali ("my wrestling," or "wrestlings of God," i.e., great/mighty wrestlings); the "wrestlings" expressing the contest (rivalry) with Leah for children and her husband's favor; Rachel claiming to have prevailed (by the two sons of Bilhah); the name commemorating the struggle.

On "wrestlings of God" (the Hebrew), the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the phrase. Clarke and Ellicott note that the Hebrew ("wrestlings of God") may mean great/mighty wrestlings (the divine name used as a superlative), or wrestlings before God (in prayer); the great contest; the rivalry, possibly joined with prayer; the struggle of the sisters.

On "I have prevailed," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the rivalry: "I have prevailed" — Rachel reckoned she had prevailed in the contest with Leah (by Bilhah's sons); the spirit of rivalry (not wholly commendable); the struggle for children and favor; the name Naphtali commemorating the wrestling; the tribe of Naphtali. The verse displays the naming of Naphtali.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the name — "With great wrestlings... I have prevailed: and she called his name Naphtali," the rivalry named (Ellicott, Gill); "wrestlings of God," the great/mighty wrestlings (Clarke, Ellicott); "I have prevailed," the contest with Leah (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the naming Rachel. The verse marks the name: Naphtali. The verse displays the wrestlings: great. The verse displays the rivalry: with Leah. The verse displays the prevailing: claimed. The naming displays Rachel's spirit of rivalry. The verse displays the Rachel: naming. The verse displays the Naphtali: wrestling. The verse displays the contest: with her sister. The naming of Naphtali displays Rachel's wrestlings with her sister: with great wrestlings have I prevailed; she called him Naphtali (my wrestling) — the rivalry with Leah for children and favor, the name commemorating the struggle, the tribe of Naphtali. The verse displays the naming Rachel, answered by Leah's expedient (v.9).
9:
"When Leah saw that she had left bearing, she took Zilpah her maid, and gave her Jacob to wife." The witnesses note Leah giving Zilpah to bear children. On "When Leah saw that she had left bearing," the grammatical voices note the occasion. Gill and Calvin note that "When Leah saw that she had left bearing, she took Zilpah her maid, and gave her Jacob to wife" — Leah, having ceased bearing (after four sons, 29:35), and seeing Rachel's expedient (vv.3-8), gave her own handmaid Zilpah to Jacob to bear more children; the rivalry continued; Leah matching Rachel's resort to the handmaid; the impatient expedient.

On "she took Zilpah her maid," the witnesses note the handmaid. Gill notes that Leah gave Zilpah (her handmaid, 29:24) to Jacob — to bear children reckoned to Leah; the same expedient as Rachel's (v.3); the rivalry of the sisters; the handmaid given.

On "gave her Jacob to wife," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the rivalry: "gave her Jacob to wife" — Zilpah given as a secondary wife (as Bilhah, v.4); the children (Gad, Asher, vv.10-13) reckoned to Leah; the contest for children carried on; the house of Israel growing through the handmaids; the rivalry (not commendable, yet overruled). The verse displays Leah giving Zilpah to bear children.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the expedient — "When Leah saw that she had left bearing, she took Zilpah her maid, and gave her Jacob to wife," Leah matching Rachel (29:35; vv.3-8, Gill, Calvin); "she took Zilpah her maid," the handmaid (29:24, Gill); "gave her Jacob to wife," the rivalry continued (vv.10-13, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the expedient Leah. The verse marks the bearing: left off. The verse displays the maid: Zilpah. The verse displays the wife: secondary. The verse displays the rivalry: with Rachel. The expedient displays Leah matching Rachel's resort. The verse displays the Leah: giving Zilpah. The verse displays the children: to come. The verse displays the contest: continued. Leah giving Zilpah to bear children displays the rivalry continued: Leah, having left bearing, gave Zilpah to Jacob (29:24; vv.3-8) — matching Rachel's expedient, the children (Gad, Asher) reckoned to Leah, the contest for children carried on. The verse displays the expedient Leah, with Zilpah's son Gad (vv.10-11).
10:
"And Zilpah Leah's maid bare Jacob a son." The very brief verse notes Zilpah bearing Jacob a son. The grammatical voices note the conception. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Zilpah Leah's maid bare Jacob a son" — Zilpah (Leah's handmaid) bore Jacob a son (Gad, v.11); the first child of Leah's handmaid, reckoned to Leah (v.9); the house of Israel growing; the providence multiplying the seed.

On "Zilpah Leah's maid," the witnesses note the handmaid. Gill notes that Zilpah bore for Leah — the handmaid's child reckoned to the mistress; the rivalry's fruit; the second handmaid become a mother of the tribes (Gad, Asher, vv.11-13).

On "bare Jacob a son," the grammatical-historical note the son: "bare Jacob a son" — the first of Zilpah's two sons (Gad, v.11); the son reckoned to Leah; the tribe of Gad; the gathering of the twelve. The verse displays Zilpah bearing Jacob a son.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the conception — "Zilpah Leah's maid bare Jacob a son," Zilpah's first son (v.11, Keil, Gill); "Zilpah Leah's maid," the handmaid (Gill); "bare Jacob a son," Gad, reckoned to Leah (v.9, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the bearing Zilpah. The verse marks the maid: Zilpah. The verse displays the son: born. The verse displays the Leah: his mother by adoption. The verse displays the tribes: growing. The conception displays the handmaid bearing for Leah. The verse displays the Zilpah: bearing. The verse displays the son: Jacob's. The verse displays the house: building. Zilpah bearing Jacob a son displays the handmaid's fruitfulness for Leah: Zilpah bare Jacob a son (Gad, v.11) — the child reckoned to Leah (v.9), the house of Israel growing through the second handmaid. The verse displays the bearing Zilpah, whose son Leah names Gad (v.11).
11:
"And Leah said, A troop cometh: and she called his name Gad." The very brief verse notes the naming of Gad. The grammatical voices note the name. Gill and Clarke note that "Leah said, A troop cometh: and she called his name Gad" — Leah named the son Gad ("a troop," or "good fortune"); the name (the Hebrew read two ways) commemorating either "a troop cometh" (the increase of children, a company coming) or "good fortune" (fortune is come, the sense of good luck/blessing); the joy at the added son.

On "A troop cometh," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the readings. Clarke and Gill note the two senses of "Gad" — "a troop/company" (the multiplying of children, a band coming) or "fortune/good luck" (Ba-gad, "with fortune," or "fortune is come"); the name expressing Leah's sense of increase and blessing; the joy at the son.

On "she called his name Gad," the grammatical-historical note the tribe: "she called his name Gad" — the son named Gad (a troop/fortune); the first of Zilpah's two sons; the tribe of Gad; the house of Israel growing. The verse displays the naming of Gad.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the name — "A troop cometh: and she called his name Gad," the name of increase/fortune (Gill, Clarke); "A troop cometh," the two readings (Clarke, Gill); "Gad," the tribe (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the naming Leah. The verse marks the name: Gad. The verse displays the troop: cometh. The verse displays the increase: joyed in. The verse displays the tribe: to come. The naming displays Leah's joy at the added son. The verse displays the Leah: naming. The verse displays the Gad: a troop. The verse displays the increase: blessed. The naming of Gad displays Leah's joy at the increase: a troop cometh; she called him Gad (a troop, or fortune) — the joy at the added son, the tribe of Gad, the house of Israel growing. The verse displays the naming Leah, with Zilpah's second son (vv.12-13).
12:
"And Zilpah Leah's maid bare Jacob a second son." The very brief verse notes Zilpah bearing a second son. The grammatical voices note the conception. Gill notes that "Zilpah Leah's maid bare Jacob a second son" — Zilpah bore Jacob a second son (Asher, v.13); the second child of Leah's handmaid, reckoned to Leah (v.9); the house of Israel growing; the providence multiplying the seed through the handmaid.

On "Zilpah Leah's maid," the witnesses note the handmaid. Gill notes that Zilpah bore again for Leah — the handmaid's second child; the rivalry's fruit; the second of Zilpah's two sons.

On "bare Jacob a second son," the grammatical-historical note the son: "bare Jacob a second son" — Asher (v.13); the second son of the handmaid; the tribe of Asher; the gathering of the twelve. The verse displays Zilpah bearing a second son.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the conception — "Zilpah Leah's maid bare Jacob a second son," Zilpah's second son (v.13, Gill); "Zilpah Leah's maid," the handmaid (Gill); "bare Jacob a second son," Asher (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the bearing Zilpah. The verse marks the maid: Zilpah. The verse displays the son: second. The verse displays the Asher: to come. The verse displays the tribes: growing. The conception displays the handmaid bearing again for Leah. The verse displays the Zilpah: bearing. The verse displays the son: second. The verse displays the house: building. Zilpah bearing a second son displays the handmaid's continued fruitfulness: Zilpah bare Jacob a second son (Asher, v.13) — the second child reckoned to Leah, the house of Israel growing. The verse displays the bearing Zilpah, whose son Leah names Asher (v.13).
13:
"And Leah said, Happy am I, for the daughters will call me blessed: and she called his name Asher." The very brief verse notes the naming of Asher. The grammatical voices note the name. Gill and Clarke note that "Leah said, Happy am I, for the daughters will call me blessed: and she called his name Asher" — Leah named the son Asher ("happy," or "blessed"); the name commemorating her happiness/blessedness in the added son; "the daughters (women) will call me blessed" (count me happy, as a fruitful mother); the joy at the son.

On "Happy am I, for the daughters will call me blessed," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the blessedness. Clarke and Gill note that Leah counted herself happy and blessed (the women would call her so) — the joy of the fruitful mother; the sense of blessing (children a heritage of the LORD, Psalm 127:3); the happiness Leah found in her sons (cf. Luke 1:48, all generations shall call me blessed).

On "she called his name Asher," the grammatical-historical note the tribe: "she called his name Asher (happy/blessed)" — the son named for Leah's happiness; the second of Zilpah's two sons; the tribe of Asher; the house of Israel growing. The verse displays the naming of Asher.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the name — "Happy am I, for the daughters will call me blessed: and she called his name Asher," the name of happiness (Gill, Clarke); "the daughters will call me blessed," the blessedness (Psalm 127:3, Clarke, Gill); "Asher," the tribe (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the naming Leah. The verse marks the name: Asher. The verse displays the happiness: counted blessed. The verse displays the daughters: will call me blessed. The verse displays the tribe: to come. The naming displays Leah's joy and sense of blessing. The verse displays the Leah: naming. The verse displays the Asher: happy. The verse displays the blessedness: owned. The naming of Asher displays Leah's happiness: happy am I, the daughters will call me blessed; she called him Asher (happy) — the joy of the fruitful mother (Psalm 127:3), the tribe of Asher. The verse displays the naming Leah, before the mandrakes (v.14).
14:
"And Reuben went in the days of wheat harvest, and found mandrakes in the field, and brought them unto his mother Leah. Then Rachel said to Leah, Give me, I pray thee, of thy son's mandrakes." The witnesses note the mandrakes and Rachel's request — the crux of the dudaim. On "Reuben... found mandrakes in the field, and brought them unto his mother Leah," the grammatical voices note the mandrakes. Ellicott and Gill note that "Reuben... found mandrakes (Hebrew dudaim) in the field" — Reuben (Leah's eldest, a child) found mandrakes at wheat harvest and brought them to Leah; the dudaim ("love-apples") variously identified: the mandrake (a plant whose fruit and root were anciently reputed to promote love and fertility), or some other fruit/flower (jasmine, citron, or fragrant blossoms — the witnesses note the identification is uncertain); the plants prized (by some) for their supposed power to aid conception.

On the mandrakes and conception, interpreters weigh the crux. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the supposed virtue of the dudaim (to promote fertility); yet the witnesses are careful: the mandrakes did not in fact give the children — for it was Leah (not Rachel) who, after the bargain, conceived (vv.17-18), while Rachel, who got the mandrakes, remained barren until "God remembered Rachel" (v.22); thus the narrative pointedly shows that fruitfulness was of God, not of the mandrakes (the supposed remedy failing where God had not given, succeeding only where God gave); the witnesses warn against the superstition that ascribed conception to the plant, when it was God's gift (v.22; Psalm 127:3).

On "Give me, I pray thee, of thy son's mandrakes," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note Rachel's request: Rachel, still barren, desired the mandrakes (hoping, it seems, they might help her conceive — the very superstition the story refutes); the request that led to the bargain (v.15) and Leah's bearing (vv.16-18); the irony that Rachel got the mandrakes but not (thereby) the child. The verse displays the mandrakes and Rachel's request.

The witnesses agree the substance: Reuben found mandrakes, and Rachel asked Leah for them (Ellicott, Calvin, Wesley, Gill). The dudaim bear a crux: the "mandrakes" (love-apples), variously identified, anciently reputed to promote fertility — yet the narrative refutes the superstition, for Rachel, who got them, stayed barren till God remembered her (v.22), while Leah (without them) conceived (vv.17-18); fruitfulness is of God, not the plant (Psalm 127:3). The verse displays the mandrakes crux. The verse marks the mandrakes: found. The verse displays the debate: the supposed virtue refuted. The verse displays the Rachel: requesting. The verse displays the gift: of God, not the plant. The mandrakes display the superstition the story refutes. The verse displays the Reuben: finding. The verse displays the dudaim: prized. The verse displays the God: who gives. The mandrakes and Rachel's request display a crux: Reuben found mandrakes, and Rachel asked for them — the dudaim (love-apples) reputed to aid fertility, yet the narrative refuting it (Rachel got them but stayed barren till God remembered her, v.22; Leah without them conceived, vv.17-18), fruitfulness being of God (Psalm 127:3). The verse displays the bargaining sisters (v.15).
15:
"And she said unto her, Is it a small matter that thou hast taken my husband? and wouldest thou take away my son's mandrakes also? And Rachel said, Therefore he shall lie with thee to night for thy son's mandrakes." The witnesses note the bargain over the mandrakes and the husband. On "Is it a small matter that thou hast taken my husband?" the grammatical voices note Leah's complaint. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Is it a small matter that thou hast taken my husband? and wouldest thou take away my son's mandrakes also?" — Leah complained that Rachel had taken (engrossed) her husband's love (Jacob loving Rachel more, 29:30); and now would she take the mandrakes too? the bitterness of the unloved wife (29:31); the rivalry over the husband's affection; the complaint of the slighted Leah.

On "thou hast taken my husband," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the grievance. Calvin and Gill note Leah's grievance — that Rachel had the chief place in Jacob's affection (29:30); the sad fruit of the rivalry; the unloved wife's bitterness; the contest for the husband.

On "he shall lie with thee to night for thy son's mandrakes," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the bargain: Rachel bargained — Leah might have Jacob that night in exchange for the mandrakes; the sad spectacle of the sisters bargaining over their husband; the rivalry reduced to a trade; the bargain that (by God's overruling) led to Leah's conceiving Issachar (vv.16-18); the providence working even through the unseemly contest. The verse displays the bargain over the mandrakes and the husband.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the complaint — "Is it a small matter that thou hast taken my husband?" Leah's grievance (29:30, Keil, Gill); "thou hast taken my husband," the rivalry over affection (Calvin, Gill); "he shall lie with thee to night for thy son's mandrakes," the bargain (vv.16-18, Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the bargaining sisters. The verse marks the complaint: taken my husband. The verse displays the mandrakes: bargained. The verse displays the rivalry: over Jacob. The verse displays the providence: overruling. The bargain displays the sisters trading over their husband. The verse displays the Leah: complaining. The verse displays the Rachel: bargaining. The verse displays the contest: reduced to trade. The bargain over the mandrakes and the husband displays the rivalry: hast thou taken my husband, and wouldst take the mandrakes too? he shall lie with thee for the mandrakes — the sisters bargaining over Jacob (29:30), the contest that, by God's overruling, led to Leah's conceiving (vv.16-18). The verse displays the bargaining Leah, who meets Jacob (v.16).
16:
"And Jacob came out of the field in the evening, and Leah went out to meet him, and said, Thou must come in unto me; for surely I have hired thee with my son's mandrakes. And he lay with her that night." The witnesses note Leah meeting Jacob, having hired him. On "Leah went out to meet him, and said, Thou must come in unto me," the grammatical voices note the meeting. Gill and Clarke note that "Leah went out to meet him, and said, Thou must come in unto me; for surely I have hired thee with my son's mandrakes" — Leah met Jacob and claimed him for the night (by the bargain, v.15); the unusual claim of the wife (the rivalry's strange fruit); the "hiring" of the husband; the bargain carried out.

On "I have hired thee with my son's mandrakes," the witnesses note the hiring. Gill notes the "hiring" — Leah had bought Jacob's company for the night with the mandrakes (v.15); the sad reduction of the marriage to a bargain; the rivalry's unseemly fruit; yet the occasion of Issachar's conception (vv.17-18, whose name reflects the "hire").

On "he lay with her that night," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the providence: "he lay with her that night" — Jacob complied; the bargain's result; God overruling the strange contest to give Leah another son (Issachar, vv.17-18); the providence working through the rivalry to build the house of Israel; the conception that followed. The verse displays Leah meeting Jacob, having hired him.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the meeting — "Leah went out to meet him... I have hired thee with my son's mandrakes," Leah claiming Jacob by the bargain (v.15, Gill, Clarke); "I have hired thee," the hiring (Gill); "he lay with her that night," the providence (vv.17-18, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the meeting Leah. The verse marks the meeting: went out. The verse displays the hire: the mandrakes. The verse displays the husband: claimed. The verse displays the providence: overruling. The meeting displays Leah claiming Jacob by the bargain. The verse displays the Leah: meeting. The verse displays the Jacob: hired. The verse displays the conception: to follow. Leah meeting Jacob, having hired him, displays the bargain's result: I have hired thee with the mandrakes; he lay with her (v.15) — the strange fruit of the rivalry, the providence overruling to give Leah another son (Issachar, vv.17-18). The verse displays the conceiving Leah (v.17).
17:
"And God hearkened unto Leah, and she conceived, and bare Jacob the fifth son." The witnesses note God hearkening to Leah, who bears a fifth son. On "God hearkened unto Leah," the grammatical voices note the hearing. Gill and Calvin note that "God hearkened unto Leah, and she conceived, and bare Jacob the fifth son" — God heard Leah (her prayer/desire) and gave her conception; the fifth son (Issachar, v.18); the conception ascribed to God (not the mandrakes, v.14) — for it was Leah, who gave up the mandrakes, who conceived, not Rachel who got them; the gift of God in answer to Leah.

On "God hearkened unto Leah," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the providence. Calvin and Gill note that the conception was of God ("God hearkened") — the pointed lesson that fruitfulness is God's gift (Psalm 127:3), not the mandrakes' (v.14); Leah's son the answer to her prayer; the providence (not the superstition) giving the child.

On "bare Jacob the fifth son," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the son: "bare Jacob the fifth son" — Issachar (v.18), Leah's fifth son; the tribe of Issachar; the house of Israel growing; the gift of God. The verse displays God hearkening to Leah, who bears a fifth son.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the hearing — "God hearkened unto Leah, and she conceived," the conception of God (v.14, Gill, Calvin); "God hearkened unto Leah," the gift, not the mandrakes (Psalm 127:3, Calvin, Gill); "bare Jacob the fifth son," Issachar (v.18, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the bearing Leah. The verse marks the hearing: of Leah. The verse displays the conception: of God. The verse displays the fifth son: born. The verse displays the mandrakes: not the cause. The hearing displays God giving Leah the child. The verse displays the God: hearkening. The verse displays the Leah: bearing. The verse displays the gift: of God. God hearkening to Leah, who bears a fifth son, displays the gift of God (not the mandrakes): God hearkened unto Leah, and she bare a fifth son (Issachar, v.18) — the conception of God, not the mandrakes (v.14; Psalm 127:3), the answer to Leah. The verse displays the bearing Leah, who names Issachar (v.18).
18:
"And Leah said, God hath given me my hire, because I have given my maiden to my husband: and she called his name Issachar." The witnesses note the naming of Issachar. On "God hath given me my hire," the grammatical voices note the name. Ellicott and Gill note that "Leah said, God hath given me my hire... and she called his name Issachar" — Leah named the son Issachar ("he brings hire," or "there is reward/hire"); the name commemorating that God had given her a "hire" (reward) — either for giving her maid Zilpah to Jacob (v.9), or recalling the "hiring" of Jacob with the mandrakes (v.16); the reward owned as God's gift.

On "because I have given my maiden to my husband," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the reason. Calvin and Gill note Leah's reckoning — that God had rewarded her for giving Zilpah to Jacob (v.9); the "hire" (reward) ascribed to God; (the witnesses note the imperfect reasoning, yet the owning of God's gift); the name reflecting the reward.

On "she called his name Issachar," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the tribe: "she called his name Issachar (hire/reward)" — the fifth son named for the reward; the tribe of Issachar; the house of Israel growing; the gift of God owned. The verse displays the naming of Issachar.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the name — "God hath given me my hire... she called his name Issachar," the reward owned (Ellicott, Gill); "because I have given my maiden to my husband," the reason (v.9, Calvin, Gill); "Issachar (hire)," the tribe (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the naming Leah. The verse marks the name: Issachar. The verse displays the hire: given of God. The verse displays the maiden: given to Jacob. The verse displays the tribe: to come. The naming displays Leah owning God's reward. The verse displays the Leah: naming. The verse displays the Issachar: hire. The verse displays the reward: God's gift. The naming of Issachar displays God's reward: God hath given me my hire; she called him Issachar (hire) — the reward (for giving Zilpah, v.9, or the hire of v.16) owned as God's gift, the tribe of Issachar. The verse displays the naming Leah, who bears Zebulun (vv.19-20).
19:
"And Leah conceived again, and bare Jacob the sixth son." The very brief verse notes Leah bearing a sixth son. The grammatical voices note the conception. Gill notes that "Leah conceived again, and bare Jacob the sixth son" — Leah conceived again and bore Jacob a sixth son (Zebulun, v.20); the sixth of her own sons; the house of Israel growing; the fruitfulness of Leah (now six sons of her own, besides the two of Zilpah).

On "conceived again," the witnesses note the fruitfulness. Gill notes that Leah conceived again — the continued fruitfulness of the once-unloved wife (29:31); the gift of God; the sixth son.

On "bare Jacob the sixth son," the grammatical-historical note the son: "bare Jacob the sixth son" — Zebulun (v.20); the sixth son of Leah; the tribe of Zebulun; the gathering of the twelve. The verse displays Leah bearing a sixth son.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the conception — "Leah conceived again, and bare Jacob the sixth son," Leah's sixth son (v.20, Gill); "conceived again," the fruitfulness (29:31, Gill); "the sixth son," Zebulun (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the bearing Leah. The verse marks the conception: again. The verse displays the son: sixth. The verse displays the Zebulun: to come. The verse displays the tribes: growing. The conception displays Leah's continued fruitfulness. The verse displays the Leah: bearing. The verse displays the son: sixth. The verse displays the house: building. Leah bearing a sixth son displays her continued fruitfulness: Leah bare Jacob the sixth son (Zebulun, v.20) — the once-unloved wife (29:31) fruitful with six sons of her own, the house of Israel growing. The verse displays the bearing Leah, who names Zebulun (v.20).
20:
"And Leah said, God hath endued me with a good dowry; now will my husband dwell with me, because I have born him six sons: and she called his name Zebulun." The witnesses note the naming of Zebulun and Leah's hope of dwelling with her husband. On "God hath endued me with a good dowry," the grammatical voices note the name. Ellicott and Gill note that "Leah said, God hath endued me with a good dowry... and she called his name Zebulun" — Leah named the son Zebulun ("dwelling," from her hope her husband would dwell with her; there is also a play on a word for "endued/gifted"); the "good dowry" (the gift of six sons) owned as God's; the name commemorating both the gift and the hope.

On "now will my husband dwell with me, because I have born him six sons," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the hope. Calvin and Gill note Leah's hope — that now, having borne six sons, her husband would "dwell with" (cleave to, be attached to) her; the continued longing of the once-unloved wife for Jacob's affection (as with Reuben, Levi, 29:32-34); the hope renewed; the craving for the husband's love.

On "she called his name Zebulun," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the tribe: "she called his name Zebulun (dwelling)" — the sixth son named for the hope of dwelling; the tribe of Zebulun; the house of Israel growing; the gift of God and the hope of the wife. The verse displays the naming of Zebulun and Leah's hope.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the name — "God hath endued me with a good dowry... she called his name Zebulun," the gift owned (Ellicott, Gill); "now will my husband dwell with me," the hope (29:32-34, Calvin, Gill); "Zebulun (dwelling)," the tribe (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the naming Leah. The verse marks the name: Zebulun. The verse displays the dowry: good. The verse displays the hope: my husband will dwell. The verse displays the tribe: to come. The naming displays Leah's gift owned and hope renewed. The verse displays the Leah: naming. The verse displays the Zebulun: dwelling. The verse displays the hope: for love. The naming of Zebulun and Leah's hope display the gift and the longing: God hath endued me with a good dowry; now will my husband dwell with me; she called him Zebulun (dwelling) — the six sons owned as God's gift, the hope (29:32-34) that Jacob would now cleave to her. The verse displays the naming Leah, who bears Dinah (v.21).
21:
"And afterwards she bare a daughter, and called her name Dinah." The very brief verse notes the birth of Dinah. The grammatical voices note the daughter. Ellicott and Gill note that "afterwards she bare a daughter, and called her name Dinah" — Leah bore a daughter, Dinah ("judgment," the feminine of Dan, or "judged/vindicated"); the only daughter of Jacob named in the record (he had other daughters, 37:35, but Dinah alone is named, for the history of ch. 34); the daughter whose later violation (ch. 34) the name perhaps anticipates.

On "she bare a daughter," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the child. Calvin and Gill note that Leah bore Dinah — a daughter (named, unlike Jacob's other daughters, for the history of ch. 34); the child of Leah; the household of Jacob.

On "called her name Dinah," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the name: "called her name Dinah (judgment)" — the daughter named Dinah; the name (perhaps anticipating her cause, ch. 34); the only named daughter; the household of Jacob complete (eleven sons and a daughter so far, before Benjamin). The verse displays the birth of Dinah.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the daughter — "she bare a daughter, and called her name Dinah," Leah's daughter (ch. 34, Ellicott, Gill); "she bare a daughter," the child (Calvin, Gill); "Dinah (judgment)," the named daughter (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the born Dinah. The verse marks the daughter: born. The verse displays the name: Dinah. The verse displays the history: ch. 34. The verse displays the household: of Jacob. The birth displays Leah's daughter, named for the coming history. The verse displays the Leah: bearing. The verse displays the Dinah: named. The verse displays the daughter: of Jacob. The birth of Dinah displays the only named daughter of Jacob: Leah bare a daughter, Dinah (judgment) — named (unlike his other daughters, 37:35) for the history of ch. 34. The verse displays the household of Jacob, before God remembers Rachel (v.22).
22:
"And God remembered Rachel, and God hearkened to her, and opened her womb." The witnesses note God remembering Rachel and opening her womb — the crux of God's remembering. On "God remembered Rachel," interpreters expound the remembering. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and the dominant reading note that "God remembered Rachel" — not that God had forgotten her (he forgets none of his), but the anthropomorphism marks the effect produced: God now acted for Rachel, regarded her, and gave what he had long withheld; "the memory of God is named from the effect produced"; the turning-point — after all the rivalry, the handmaids, and the mandrakes (which gave nothing, v.14), God himself, in his own time, remembered the barren Rachel and gave her a child; the gift wholly of God.

On "God hearkened to her," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the prayer. Calvin and Gill note that "God hearkened to her" — Rachel had at last sought God by prayer (her early impatience, vv.1-2, giving way to prayer); her prayer and faith obtained what envy and the mandrakes could not; the answered prayer; the lesson that what is sought of God (not of man or means) is given in his time; the prayer heard.

On "opened her womb," the grammatical-historical note the gift: "opened her womb" — God gave Rachel conception (the barren made fruitful, as Sarah, Rebekah, and later Hannah); the gift of God alone (Psalm 127:3); the birth of Joseph (vv.23-24); the long-barren Rachel at last a mother; the providence in God's own time. The verse displays God remembering Rachel and opening her womb.

The witnesses agree the substance: God remembered Rachel, heard her, and gave her a child (Clarke, Calvin, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of God's remembering: "God remembered Rachel" — the anthropomorphism marking that God now acted for her (he had not forgotten); after the rivalry, the handmaids, and the mandrakes (which gave nothing, v.14), God himself, in his own time and in answer to prayer, gave the barren Rachel a child (Joseph); the gift wholly of God (Psalm 127:3). The verse displays the God-remembered crux. The verse marks the remembering: of Rachel. The verse displays the hearing: her prayer. The verse displays the womb: opened. The verse displays the gift: of God. The remembering displays God giving in his own time. The verse displays the God: remembering. The verse displays the Rachel: heard. The verse displays the child: given. God remembering Rachel and opening her womb display the crux: God remembered Rachel, hearkened to her, and opened her womb — the anthropomorphism marking God's acting for her, the gift (after the mandrakes gave nothing, v.14) wholly of God, in his own time and in answer to prayer (Psalm 127:3). The verse displays the conceiving Rachel, who bears Joseph (vv.23-24).
23:
"And she conceived, and bare a son; and said, God hath taken away my reproach:" The witnesses note Rachel bearing a son and her reproach taken away. On "she conceived, and bare a son," the grammatical voices note the birth. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "she conceived, and bare a son; and said, God hath taken away my reproach" — Rachel, remembered by God (v.22), conceived and bore a son (Joseph, v.24); the long-barren wife at last a mother; the gift of God; the joy of the answered prayer.

On "God hath taken away my reproach," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the reproach. Calvin and Gill note that Rachel said "God hath taken away my reproach" — the reproach of barrenness (childlessness was reckoned a reproach in Israel, as for Sarah, Rebekah, Hannah, Elisabeth, Luke 1:25); God had removed it by giving her a son; the relief and joy; the reproach lifted by God's gift.

On "my reproach," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the gift: "God hath taken away my reproach" — the barrenness (the reproach) removed by the gift of a son; God's hand owned; the joy of the once-barren wife; the birth that would be named Joseph (v.24). The verse displays Rachel bearing a son and her reproach taken away.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the birth — "she conceived, and bare a son," Rachel's son (v.22, Keil, Gill); "God hath taken away my reproach," the reproach of barrenness removed (Luke 1:25, Calvin, Gill); the gift of God (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the bearing Rachel. The verse marks the son: born. The verse displays the reproach: taken away. The verse displays the God: who gave. The verse displays the joy: of the mother. The birth displays Rachel's reproach removed by God. The verse displays the Rachel: bearing. The verse displays the reproach: lifted. The verse displays the gift: of God. Rachel bearing a son and her reproach taken away display the gift of God: she bare a son, and said, God hath taken away my reproach (v.22) — the reproach of barrenness (Luke 1:25) removed by God's gift, the once-barren wife at last a mother. The verse displays the rejoicing Rachel, who names Joseph (v.24).
24:
"And she called his name Joseph; and said, The LORD shall add to me another son." The witnesses note the naming of Joseph and Rachel's hope of another son. On "she called his name Joseph," the grammatical voices note the name. Ellicott and Gill note that "she called his name Joseph; and said, The LORD shall add to me another son" — Rachel named the son Joseph ("he shall add," or "adding"); the name commemorating both the taking away of her reproach (v.23, by a play, "asaph," gathered/taken away) and the hope that the LORD would "add" (yasaph) another son; the double sense in the name; the son Joseph, the beloved, the future deliverer of his house (chs. 37-50).

On "The LORD shall add to me another son," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the hope. Clarke and Gill note Rachel's faith/hope — "the LORD shall add to me another son"; the expectation of further blessing (fulfilled in Benjamin, 35:18, at whose birth she would die); the hope of the once-barren wife; the faith looking for more of God's gift.

On the name, the reformed and grammatical-historical note Joseph: "she called his name Joseph" — the son of Rachel, the beloved; the future saviour of Jacob's house in Egypt (chs. 37-50); a notable type, by many, of Christ (the beloved son, rejected by his brethren, exalted to save); the tribe(s) of Joseph (Ephraim and Manasseh); the gift of God to Rachel. The verse displays the naming of Joseph and Rachel's hope.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the name — "she called his name Joseph... The LORD shall add to me another son," the name and the hope (Ellicott, Gill); "Joseph (he shall add)," the double sense (v.23, Gill); "The LORD shall add to me another son," the hope (35:18, Clarke, Gill); Joseph the future saviour and type of Christ (chs. 37-50, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the naming Rachel. The verse marks the name: Joseph. The verse displays the hope: another son. The verse displays the Joseph: the beloved. The verse displays the type: of Christ. The naming displays Rachel's joy and hope. The verse displays the Rachel: naming. The verse displays the Joseph: he shall add. The verse displays the hope: for more. The naming of Joseph and Rachel's hope display the gift and the expectation: she called him Joseph (he shall add); the LORD shall add another son (35:18) — the reproach removed and the hope of more, the son Joseph, the beloved, the future saviour of his house and a type of Christ (chs. 37-50). The verse closes the births, before Jacob seeks to depart (v.25).
